coredata.dc:title: Copper nanoclusters @ nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots-based ratiometric fluorescence probe for lead (II) ions detection in porphyra
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Wenting
Hu, Xuetao
Li, Qian
Shi, Yongqiang
Zhai, Xiaodong
Xu, Yiwei
Li, Zhihua
Huang, Xiaowei
Wang, Xin
Shi, Jiyong
Zou, Xiaobo
Kang, Shifei
coredata.dc:description: A novel ratiometric fluorescence probe was proposed for detecting lead (II) ions (Pb2+) in porphyra, the approach was based on copper nanoclusters and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CuNCs-CNQDs). In this probe, the CuNCs delivered the response signal, the fluorescence of which was enhanced by Pb2+ due to the aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) between Pb2+ and CuNCs. The CNQDs provided the self-calibration signal, whose fluorescence remained almost unchanged in coexistence with Pb2+. According to the change of fluorescence intensity ratio between the fluorophores, CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid was used as ratiometric probes for the sensitive detection of Pb2+ in the range of 0.010–2.5 mg L−1, with a detection limit of 0.0031 mg L−1. Finally, the probe was successfully applied to detect Pb2+ in porphyra with relative standard deviations (RSDs) lower than 5%. This study provides a straightforward, stable, and sensitive approach for detecting Pb2+ in porphyra.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper nanoclusters
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Aggregation-induced emission enhancement
Lead (II) ions
Ratiometric fluorescence probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of lead ions in porphyra samples
Characterization of the as-prepared CNQDs and the CuNCs-CNQDs nanohybrid
Selectivity and stability of CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid toward Pb2+
Detection of Pb2+ in porphyra samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CNQDs. Inset shows the HRTEM image of CNQDs, (B) TEM image of the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid. The inset displays the HRTEM image of the CuNCs, and (C) TEM image of the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid with Pb2+.
(A) FT-IR spectra of CNQDs and the CuNCs-CNQDs nanohybrid; (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of CNQDs, the CuNCs-CNQDs nanohybrid and the CuNCs-CNQDs nanohybrid with Pb2+ (λex = 365 nm).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid at different concentrations of Pb2+. The inset is the fluorescence images. (B) Linear relationship between the response value (I632/I468) of the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid and the Pb2+ concentration (0.010–2.5 mg L−1) (N = 3). (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CuNCs at different concentrations of Pb2+. The inset displays the fluorescence images. (D) Linear relationship between the response value ((I-I0)/I0) of CuNCs and the Pb2+ concentration (0.29–1.86 mg L−1) (N = 3).

(A) Selectivity of the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid probe in the presence of various metal ions. The concentration of Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, K+, Na+, Zn2+ Cu2+, and Fe2+ was 6 mg L−1. The concentration of Pb2+ was 2 mg L−1; (B) Deviation rate of the detection results of the CuNCs and the CuNCs-CNQDs nanohybrid probes at the different measurement temperatures (N = 6).
Schematic of the preparation of (A) CNQDs and (B) the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid. (C) Schematic for the Pb2+ detection assay.
Pb2+ concentration in porphyra samples detected using the ICP-MS and the fluorescence method.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: bPaired t-test: P < 0.05, significant difference; P > 0.05, no significant difference.

head.ce:title: Copper nanoclusters @ nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots-based ratiometric fluorescence probe for lead (II) ions detection in porphyra

School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The first two authors contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel ratiometric fluorescence probe was proposed for detecting lead (II) ions (Pb2+) in porphyra, the approach was based on copper nanoclusters and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CuNCs-CNQDs). In this probe, the CuNCs delivered the response signal, the fluorescence of which was enhanced by Pb2+ due to the aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) between Pb2+ and CuNCs. The CNQDs provided the self-calibration signal, whose fluorescence remained almost unchanged in coexistence with Pb2+. According to the change of fluorescence intensity ratio between the fluorophores, CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid was used as ratiometric probes for the sensitive detection of Pb2+ in the range of 0.010–2.5 mg L−1, with a detection limit of 0.0031 mg L−1. Finally, the probe was successfully applied to detect Pb2+ in porphyra with relative standard deviations (RSDs) lower than 5%. This study provides a straightforward, stable, and sensitive approach for detecting Pb2+ in porphyra.

ce:section.ce:para: Porphyra is an edible algae known for its unique flavor, high nutritional value, and medicinal properties. Porphyra is rich in bioactive substances, such as proteins, amino acids, lipids, mineral salts, polyphenols, and polysaccharides. Its protein concentration can be as high as 20–30% (dry weight), and the composition of its amino acids conforms to the recommended reference pattern of the Food and Agriculture Organization and the World Health Organization (Chen, Wu, Yang, & Wang, 2017; Rubio et al., 2017). Research shows that porphyra can effectively reduce the incidence of cancer, cardiovascular, and heart diseases (Déléris, Nazih, & Bard, 2016). Nevertheless, Scholars have warned of the presence of heavy metals (lead, mercury, and cadmium) in porphyra as a result of increasing wastewater pollution (Carpenter, Arcaro, & Spink, 2002; Chen et al., 2018; Raize, Argaman, & Yannai, 2004). Lead ions (Pb2+) are hypertoxic and can be transmitted and concentrated through the food chain (Liu, Wang, Zhang, Hou, & Liu, 2016; Zhang et al., 2018). Numerous studies have proven that even low-level exposure to Pb2+ can lead to serious health hazards, especially intellectual disabilities in children (Han et al., 2017; Llobett, Falco, Casas, Teixido, & Domingo, 2003). Therefore, the determination of Pb2+ levels in porphyra is a crucial research topic for safety assessments and nutritional value studies.

Several techniques are typically used to detect trace amounts of Pb2+, such as atomic fluorescence spectroscopy (AFS) (Wan, Xu, & Wang, 2006), inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (Cao, Deng, Yan, & Huang, 2017), atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) (Adam & Anthemidis, 2009), ultraviolet–visible & high-performance liquid chromatography (UV–Vis & HPLC) (Thirumalai, Kumar, Prabhakaran, Sivaraman, & Maheswari, 2018), electrochemical methods (Zhang et al., 2018), and fluorescent sensor methods (Chen et al., 2018; Goswami et al., 2011; Hu, Shi, Shi, Li, et al., 2019; Wang, Hu, Zhuang, & Ni, 2016). Among these methods, fluorescent sensor methods have been praised as notably effective protocols for determining concentrations of trace metal ions; fluorescent sensor methods offer convenience, instantaneous response, effective selectivity, and high sensitivity (Hu, Shi, Shi, Zou, et al., 2019; Li et al., 2019; Wang & Wu, 2019).

Metal nanoclusters have attracted increasing interest because of their unique photoluminescent properties, such as high quantum yields, narrow and symmetric size-tunable emission, broad absorption spectra, and high resistance (Li et al., 2019; Ou et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018). Various metal nanoclusters with precious metals (gold, silver, and platinum) as precursors have been widely developed to construct fluorescent assays of Pb2+ (Chen et al., 2018; Goswami et al., 2011; Ji et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2013). Such precious-metal nanoclusters are unavoidably expensive, and their synthesis usually causes environmental pollution. In addition, in numerous fluorescence probes, a single-emission fluorescence peak is still primarily used as the signal to detect Pb2+, such probes are susceptible to environmental interference (He et al., 2018). Therefore, the development of a low-cost, interference-resistant, and environmentally friendly fluorescence probe is crucial for the detection of Pb2+ in porphyra.

Relative to precious metals, copper is affordably priced. Copper can be refined from widely available raw materials. A previous study revealed that CuNCs have aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) properties (Han et al., 2017). Thus, CuNCs can serve as the basis for low-cost, high-performance fluorescence probes for Pb2+ detection in porphyra. Moreover, in ratiometric fluorescence probes, the intensity ratio of dual-emission peaks is used for self-calibration function, which could avoid the pitfalls of probe concentrations, environmental interference, and intrinsic fluorescence. Many reports have confirmed that the detection performance (including considerations such as sensitivity, stability, and accuracy) of ratiometric fluorescence probes (dual-emission fluorescent signal) can be improved considerably (Hu et al., 2019; Li et al., 2019). Therefore, a ratio-based fluorescence probe can be developed with CuNCs to detect Pb2+. Several groups have fabricated CuNCs-based ratiometric probes, and they have been successfully applied to detect H2S (Gao, Li, Wang, & Tan, 2017), dopamine (He et al., 2018), and Cu2+ (Liu et al., 2015). However, few publications have reported the application of this strategy to detect Pb2+.

In this study, a novel ratiometric fluorescence probe based on the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid was proposed for detecting Pb2+ in prophyra. In this probe, the CuNCs provided the response signal, the fluorescence of which was enhanced by the AIEE effect between Pb2+ and CuNCs. The CNQDs provided the self-calibration signal, whose fluorescence intensity remained almost unchanged when in the presence of Pb2+. Therefore, a probe that can measure the change of fluorescence intensity ratio between the fluorophores can be composed of a CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid, and can be used as a ratiometric probe for the sensitive and selective detection of Pb2+ in porphyra samples, the proposed low-cost, interference-resistant, and environmentally friendly probe would be useful.

Cupric nitrate (Cu(NO3)2), l-glutathione reduced (GSH), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ammonium chloride (NH4Cl), hydrochloric acid (HCl), concentrated nitric acid (HNO3), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), Tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris), and sodium citrate were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). A lead standard solution was purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Beijing, China). All the reagents used in this study were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Double-distilled water was used throughout the study.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a transmission electron microscope (JEM-1400, JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was conducted using an FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet FT-IR spectrometer 5700, USA). Fluorescence spectra were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-98, Lengguang, China). Visual fluorescence images were captured in a sealed dark box configured with an ultraviolet lamp (365 nm). The actual content of Pb2+ was detected by ICP-MS (XSeries II, ThermoFisher, USA). The pretreatment of Porphyra samples was completed by Microwave Digestion System (TOP wave, Analytik Jena, Germany) and Electric hot plate (GS, Zhenghong, China).

The CNQDs were prepared using the following method (Guo, Wang, Shao, & Jiang, 2013). Briefly, sodium citrate (0.1 g), NH4Cl (0.53 g), and water (5 mL) were blended and transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. This autoclave was placed in a baking oven for 4 h at 180 °C. Then, the autoclave was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. The mixture was purified with a dialysis membrane (5000 Da) for 24 h to remove unreacted precursors. Next, the resulting product was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min to remove insoluble large-particle impurities. The purified CNQDs solution was preserved at 4 °C for further use.

The CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid was synthesized on the basis of a previous study (Han et al., 2017). Typically, GSH (62 mg) and Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (12 mg) were added to the purified CNQDs solution (10 mL), and the mixture was stirred at 25 °C to obtain a transparent solution. Then, the solution was heated at 80 °C under stirring for 10 min to form a white emulsion. Subsequently, a NaOH solution (1 mol L−1) was added drop-wise until the white emulsion gradually became a transparent pale yellow, corresponding to a pH value in the range of 4–5. At this point, the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid was successfully synthesized. The mixture was purified in darkness with a dialysis membrane (5000 Da) for 24 h to remove unreacted precursors in the dark. The purified CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid samples were preserved at 4 °C without exposure to light for further use.

Fluorescence measurement of Pb2+ was accomplished using the following steps. First, 100 μL of the as-prepared CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid was mixed with 10 μL of Pb2+ solution at different concentrations. Then, the mixture was diluted to a final volume of 1000 μL with Tris–HCl buffer (pH = 7.5) and then thoroughly shaken and mixed. Next, the mixture was incubated at 25 °C for 6 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectra were recorded using a fluorescence spectrometer (λex = 365 nm). The fluorescence intensity ratio (I632/I468) between I632 and I468 was calculated for the quantitative analysis of Pb2+ concentration. To conduct visual fluorescence detection, visual fluorescence images were captured under ultraviolet excitation (365 nm) in the sealed dark box.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of lead ions in porphyra samples
ce:section.ce:para: CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid were used to detect Pb2+ in porphyra samples. Porphyra samples (N = 5) were purchased at Kaiyuan supermarket in Zhenjiang (Jiangsu province, China). Porphyra was processed using a microwave digestion method. First, 10 g of the porphyra powder was accurately weighed and transferred into a Teflon extraction vessel. The powder was treated with 6 mL of 65% w/w HNO3 and 1 mL of 30% w/w H2O2 for 8 h with occasional shaking. Next, the Teflon extraction vessel was placed in the microwave digestion apparatus. The program adopted for the microwave-assisted digestion is presented in Table S1. Finally, the Teflon extraction vessel was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature; the mixture was transferred to a beaker. To complete the acid removal process, the mixture was evaporated on an electric hot plate at 150 °C for 30 min to remove a large amount of dissolved acid. This was done to prevent highly acidic materials from damaging the instrument (Santos et al., 2019). Deacidification solution was diluted to 10 mL with ultrapure water. All samples were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C and filtrated with a 0.22 μm syringe filter before analysis. Other than that, no special pretreatment was required. The proposed fluorescence method and the standard ICP-MS method (the optimal instrumental parameters are presented in Table S2) were used for Pb2+ determination in the aforementioned porphyra samples. Employed ICP-MS as a standard heavy metal ion detection method to validate the detection performance of the proposed fluorescence method. All the porphyra samples were tested three times using ICP-MS to minimize possible errors, and the mean value of measured data for each sample was used as the determination result.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the as-prepared CNQDs and the CuNCs-CNQDs nanohybrid
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid involved two steps. First, CNQDs were synthesized through a one-step method from sodium citrate and NH4Cl. Carbon quantum dots doped with nitrogen can effectively improve the fluorescence properties and enhance applicability (Li, Yuan, Anjana, Susan, & Zhou, 2014). Then, the obtained CNQDs and Cu2+ were used as precursors and GSH was used as a stabilizer and reducer to prepare a CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid using a hydrothermal method. With this straightforward, environmentally friendly, and low-cost method, the reaction conditions were easily achievable. The raw materials were inexpensive and not harmful (Gao et al., 2017; He et al., 2018).

The morphology and size of the CNQDs and CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid are displayed in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1(A) illustrates that CNQDs possessed an almost spherical morphology with an average diameter of 2 nm. A high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) produced a high-magnification image (inset of Fig. 1(A)) that revealed that the CNQDs had a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm, which was consistent with the typical facet (100) of graphite (Ding, Gao, & Li, 2018). The prepared CNQDs had high dispersity; they served as templates for the in situ synthesis of CuNCs (Li, Guo, Yuan, & Lu, 2017). The TEM images of the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid (Fig. 1(B)) displayed abundant particles with an average diameter of 9 nm. Fig. 1(B) reveals a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which proved the successful synthesis of CuNCs (Tang et al., 2020). More notably, a small amount of dispersed CNQDs can be observed in Fig. 1(B); the coexistence of CNQDs and CuNCs indicates the formation of the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid.

Fig. 2 (A) depicts the FT-IR spectra of the CNQDs and the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid. The CNQDs exhibited stretching vibrations of CN and NH bonds at 1420–1400 cm−1 and 3500–3100 cm−1, respectively, indicating the presence of nitrogen as a doping agent in the quantum dots (Saini et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2018). The CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid displayed a new absorption band at 1645 cm−1 that was assigned to the CN stretching vibrations. Moreover, the CN group at 1420–1400 cm−1 became weak and the 3500–3100 cm−1 of NH stretching vibrations indicated blueshift, confirming interactions between CNQDs and CuNCs in the synthesis of CuNCs (He et al., 2018). This analysis indicated the successful preparation of the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid.

The optical properties of CNQDs and the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid were characterized by fluorescence spectra. As illustrated in Fig. 2(B), CNQDs displayed a fluorescence emission maximum at 468 nm when excited at 365 nm, and the corresponding solution displayed intense blue fluorescence, which was visible to the naked eye under a 365 nm UV lamp. The CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid shown fluorescence signal with the dual-emission peaks at 468 nm and 632 nm under a 365 nm excitation. However, the fluorescence intensity of CNQDs decreased slightly, and the corresponding solution displayed pale blue fluorescence, which may have been due to the energy transfer effect between CuNCs and CNQDs (He et al., 2018). When Pb2+ was added into the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid solution, the fluorescence intensity of the CuNCs at 632 nm was markedly enhanced, whereas the fluorescence intensity of the CNQDs remained unchanged. Simultaneously, the corresponding mixture displayed orange fluorescence due to the combination of Pb2+ with CuNCs; this orange fluorescence was caused by the AIEE effect of the CuNCs (Han et al., 2017; Ji et al., 2015). The coexistence of Pb2+ and the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid changed the fluorescence signal of CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid, indicating that the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid could be used as a probe for Pb2+ detection.

The sensing mechanism by which the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid was able to detect Pb2+ was explored using TEM and a fluorescence spectrogram. As shown in Fig. 1(B) and (C), CuNCs and CNQDs had a monodispersed state in the absence of Pb2+. When Pb2+ was added into that system, CuNCs aggregated together, which could be explained by the complexation of Pb2+ with CuNCs modified with a large number of active groups from GSH (Ji et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2013). According to Pearson’s concept of hard and soft acid and base theory, Pb is a soft and intermediate-to-soft metal that is mainly involved in covalent or coordinative interactions with sulfur and nitrogen-containing ligands (Raize et al., 2004). Studies have shown that GSH has a high-affinity site for Pb2+ because GSH is rich in carboxylate, sulfhydryl, and amino groups (Ji et al., 2015). As Fig. 2. (B) shows, only the fluorescence intensity of CuNCs at 632 nm was altered in the presence of Pb2+, confirming that Pb2+ gave a boost to the AIEE of CuNCs (Liu et al., 2013). Studies have reported that the fluorescence of a fluorescence probe with AIEE characteristics was weak in a monodispersed state, but strong in an aggregated state (Ji et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2013).

AIEE sensing mechanisms have been widely adopted to fluorescence analysis (Ji et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2013). AIEE behavior is usually considered to be associated with the restriction of the intramolecular rotation process and nonplanar configuration in an aggregate state that presents slight π–π stacking interactions (An, Kwon, Jung, & Park, 2002). In this system, the aggregation of CuNCs enhanced self-fluorescence signals (response signal), whereas that of CNQDs remained stable (self-calibration signal), which may be due to the fact that the size ratio of CNQDs to CuNCs does not change the fluorescence of CNQDs despite the aggregation of CuNCs. Because the change of fluorescence intensity ratio between the fluorophores indicates the presence of Pb2+, a CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid could be used as a ratiometric probe to detect Pb2+. A detection schematic is presented in Scheme . Under the ultraviolet irradiation at 365 nm, the prepared CNQDs exhibited blue fluorescence, whereas the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid presented pale blue fluorescence due to an energy transfer effect. When Pb2+ was added into the CuNCs–CNQDs system, the fluorescence color turned orange-red owing to the AIEE effect. These results proved that this study’s methods successfully produced a novel ratiometric fluorescence probe for detecting Pb2+.

To obtain the optimal experimental results, the pH of the detection system and the reaction time of the mixture were studied. First, the effects of different pH (4, 5, 6, 7, 7.5, 8, 9, and 10) on the detection performance of Pb2+ 0.83 mg L−1 were investigated. As depicted in Fig. S1. (A), as the pH shifted from 4 to 7.5, the response value (I632/I468) increased gradually. However, I632/I468 decreased when the pH shifted from 7.5 to 10. A strong acid or alkaline environment can change the electric potential of a system, this phenomenon affected the stability of the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid probe (Xavier et al., 2017). Thus, a weak alkaline medium of pH 7.5 was optimal for the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid detection system. Further trials sought to optimize the reaction time (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 min) after the addition of Pb2+. Fig. S1. (B) graphs the influence of reaction time when the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid probe was exposed to 1.0 mg L−1 of Pb2+. The I632/I468 tended to be stable when the reaction time reached 6 min. When the reaction time was extended, the I632/I468 remained almost unchanged, indicating that the reaction of CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid with Pb2+ was almost completed at 6 min. Therefore, a reaction time of 6 min was applied in the follow-up experiments.

Under the optimal conditions, the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid ratiometric probe was used for the quantitative detection of Pb2+ concentrations. As shown in Fig. 3 (A), the fluorescent intensity at 632 nm regularly increased, whereas that of CNQDs at 468 nm was almost unaffected by the increased in Pb2+ concentration. Fig. 3(B) illustrates that I632/I468 displayed an accurate linear relationship with different Pb2+ concentrations in the range of 0.010–2.5 mg L−1. The corresponding linear regression equation is: Y = 0.2455 X + 0.2581 (R 2 = 0.9971), where Y represents I632/I468 value and X is the Pb2+ concentration. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.0031 mg L−1 according to 3σ/k (where σ denotes the standard deviation obtained by conducting 11 repeated detections of a blank solution where k is the slope of the calibration curve) (He et al., 2018). The threshold requirement for Pb2+ detection in porphyra is set by national standards at 1 mg kg−1. The LOD value was far below the highest permitted value. As summarized in Table S3, the method proposed in this study exhibited lower LOD and wider linear detection range than those observed in most previous studies. Thus, the nanohybrid is an ideal probe material for the ratiometric determination of Pb2+, its CuNCs serve as the Pb2+ recognition component and its CNQDs act as the reference component.

Various fluorescence images were captured upon addition of Pb2+ of various concentration values in the range of 0–1.9 mg L−1 under 365 nm ultraviolet irradiation. The inset in Fig. 3(A) presents the fluorescence color of the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid, the color gradually changed from blue to red with the increasing concentration of Pb2+, corresponding to the fluorescence intensity changes (the greatly-changed enhanced fluorescence peaked at 632 nm, but the barely-changed fluorescence peaked at 468 nm), indicating the concentration of Pb2+could be distinguished by the naked eye. Therefore, this ratiometric fluorescence assay could be applied for visual detection of Pb2+.

Moreover, the sensitivity of the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid to Pb2+ was compared with the sensitivity of a CuNCs fluorescence probe. As shown in Fig. 3(C) and (D), I632 gradually increased with the increase of the concentration of Pb2+. The response value ((I–I0)/ I0) of CuNCs displayed an accurate linear relationship with the Pb2+ concentrations in the range of 0.29–1.86 mg L−1. (Fig. 3. (D)), and the linear regression equation can be expressed as Y = 1.3155 X + 0.0200 (R 2 = 0.9866), where Y is I632 and X is the concentration of Pb2+. The corresponding LOD was estimated to be 0.087 mg L−1 from the equation 3σ/k. These results revealed that the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid ratiometric probe had a higher detection sensitivity and wider linear range than CuNCs fluorescence probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and stability of CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid toward Pb2+
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is a crucial parameter for evaluating the performance of the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid ratiometric probe designed to detect Pb2+. In the present study, the selectivity values of the probe to Pb2+ (at a concentration of 2 mg L−1) and to other metal ions, including Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, K+, Na+, Zn2+ Cu2+, and Fe2+, (at a concentration of 6 mg L−1) were carefully investigated. As presented in Fig. 4 (A), maximum change of I632/I468 was measured for the nanohybrid probe upon the addition of Pb2+. Moreover, the I632/I468 was almost unchanged for any other metal ions even when they were presented at much higher concentrations than Pb2+. As explained in the detection mechanism, the presence of Pb2+ facilitated the AIEE effect of the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid, whereas other metal ions had little influence on its structure. In summary, the proposed CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid probe could be employed as a selective probe for Pb2+ detection.

The stability of the fluorescence probe is another important index. This study investigated the detection stability of a dual-emission CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid probe and a single-emission CuNCs probe at different measurement temperatures. To compare the detection stability of these two probe types, fluorescence spectra were measured and recorded by controlling the system at 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, and 30 °C. Previous experiments were performed at 25 °C. Then, the deviation rates of the detection results of two types of probes were compared at different temperatures. The deviation rate formula is |C − C 0|/C 0 × 100%, where C is the Pb2+ concentration that must be measured using the fluorescence method at different temperatures, and C 0 is the reference Pb2+ concentration. As shown in Fig. 4(B), at different temperatures, the deviation rates of the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid probe and the single-emission CuNCs probe were 0.0011–0.77% and 0.17–2.1%, respectively.

Research has shown that the fluorescence properties of nanomaterials are not only influenced by their molecular structure but also by their concentration and environmental factors (He et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2018). Studies have revealed that the fluorescence intensity decreases as temperature increases. This behavior can be attributed to the increased conversion rate of electronic energy into vibrational energy (Jana, Inamdar, & HM, 2017). Therefore, any the change of fluorescence intensity resulting from a change of temperature would lead to a change in the probe’s detection deviation rate. Under the different temperatures, the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid probe had a smaller deviation rate than the single-emission CuNCs probe. This was due to the built-in self-calibration function of the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid probe. Self-calibration function can weaken the influence of temperature changes on detection results (He et al., 2018). These results confirmed that the self-calibration function of the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid ratiometric probe can effectively improve its detection stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Pb2+ in porphyra samples
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the CuNCs–CNQDs ratiometric probes were applied for the practical detection of Pb2+ in porphyra samples, and the performance levels of probes were evaluated using the ICP-MS method. As listed in Table 1 , the levels of Pb2+ in porphyra samples were in the range of 0.88–2.4 mg kg−1 and 0.85–2.3 mg kg−1, respectively, as measured by ICP-MS and the proposed fluorescence method. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the fluorescence method was <5%. The result of the paired t-test exhibited no significant difference between the fluorescence methods and the ICP-MS, this indicated that the proposed fluorescence method has high stability and accuracy for detecting Pb2+ in porphyra samples. These results suggested the ratiometric fluorescence method based on the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid has great potential for detecting dangerous heavy metals in real-world situations.

A straightforward and highly sensitive ratiometric fluorescence probe was made from the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid. This probe detected trace Pb2+ contents in porphyra samples. The CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid had dual-emission peaks at 468 nm and 632 nm, it exhibited a ratiometric fluorescence response in the presence of Pb2+ because of the AIEE characteristics of CuNCs. Under optimal conditions, the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid exhibited excellent sensitivity toward Pb2+ in the range of 0.010–2.5 mg L−1 with a detection limit of 0.0031 mg L−1. Because the dual-emission fluorescence probe was self-calibration function, the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid was found to have higher detection stability than a single-emission CuNCs probe. Finally, the CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid was successfully applied for the selective and sensitive detection of Pb2+ in porphyra samples. The results were in agreement with those obtained using the ICP-MS method, with a low RSD of 1.9%–4.5%. This study proved that CuNCs–CNQDs nanohybrid can be employed for Pb2+ detection.

Wenting Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Xuetao Hu: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Qian Li: Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Yongqiang Shi: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Xiaodong Zhai: Validation, Investigation. Yiwei Xu: Formal analysis. Zhihua Li: Software, Visualization. Xiaowei Huang: Data curation. Xin Wang: Formal analysis. Jiyong Shi: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xiaobo Zou: Supervision. Shifei Kang: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by all the funds.

This work was supported by the the National Key Research and Development Program of China (grant numbers 2017YFC1600805); the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers 31772073, 31671844); the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (grant numbers BE2019359); Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.126623.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
